1: qawwōh qiwwîtî YHWH wayyēṭ ʾēlay wayyišmaʿ šawʿātî (qawwōh qiwwîtî YHWH wayyēṭ ʾēlay wayyišmaʿ šawʿātî): 'I waited patiently for the LORD; he inclined to me and heard my cry.' The cognate infinitive absolute qawwōh qiwwîtî ('I waited, waited!') is intensified repetition — the waiting was sustained, thorough, emphatic. This is the taut expectation of qāwâ ('to hope with expectation'), not passive resignation. The divine response: wayyēṭ ʾēlay ('he inclined toward me') — the anthropomorphism of a king bending to hear a petition — and wayyišmaʿ šawʿātî ('he heard my cry'). The psalm opens with answered prayer and works backward and forward from this center of rescue.
2: wayyaʿălēnî mibbôr šāʾôn mîṭîṭ hayyāwēn wayyāqem ʿal-selaʿ raglāy kônnēn ʾăšûrāy (wayyaʿălēnî mibbôr šāʾôn mîṭîṭ hayyāwēn wayyāqem ʿal-selaʿ raglāy ḵônnēn ʾăšûrāy): 'He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog, and set my feet upon a rock, making my steps secure.' The spatial rescue from bôr šāʾôn ('the pit of roaring/destruction') and ṭîṭ hayyāwēn ('the miry clay') to selaʿ ('a rock') is the psalm's central metaphor: from chaotic instability to stable ground. The word bôr ('pit') is used for the grave/Sheol in Psalms 28:1; Psalms 30:3 — the rescue is from the edge of death. The rock (selaʿ) appears throughout the Psalter as the divine attribute of stability (Psalms 18:2, 31); here it is the literal place where the delivered one stands.
3: wayyittēn bĕpî šîr ḥādāš tĕhillâ lēʾlōhênû yirʾû rabbîm wĕyîrāʾû wĕyibṭĕḥû baYHWH (wayyittēn bĕp̄î šîr ḥādāš ṯĕhillâ lēʾlōhênû yirʾû rabbîm wĕyîrāʾû wĕyiḇṭĕḥû ḇaYHWH): 'He put a new song in my mouth, a song of praise to our God. Many will see and fear, and put their trust in the LORD.' The divine gift of šîr ḥādāš ('a new song') — the song that rescue produces is qualitatively new: it cannot be the old song because the situation is transformed. The phrase wayyittēn bĕpî ('he placed in my mouth') makes the song a divine endowment, not self-generated praise. The missionary effect: yirʾû rabbîm ('many will see') → wĕyîrāʾû ('they will fear') → wĕyibṭĕḥû baYHWH ('they will trust in the LORD'). Rescue generates testimony which generates fear of God which generates trust: a chain of effect from one person's deliverance to the expansion of the covenant community.
4: ʾašrê haggeber ʾăšer śām YHWH miṭlaḥtô wĕlōʾ-pānâ ʾel-rĕhābîm wĕśāṭê kāzāb (ʾašrê haggeber ʾăšer śām YHWH miṭlaḥtô wĕlōʾ-p̄ānâ ʾel-rĕhābîm wĕśāṭê ḵāzāb): 'Blessed is the man who makes the LORD his trust, who does not turn to the proud, to those who go astray after a lie!' The beatitude ʾašrê ('blessed!') marks the transition from personal testimony to universal instruction. The one who makes YHWH miṭlaḥtô ('his trust') — the object of utter reliance — is blessed. The negative contrasts: lōʾ-pānâ ('he does not turn') toward rĕhābîm ('the proud, arrogant') or śāṭê kāzāb ('those who go off after a lie'). The lie is the false alternative to YHWH — any competing object of trust that promises what only YHWH can give.
5: rabbôt ʿāśîtā ʾattâ YHWH ʾĕlōhay niplĕʾōtêkā wĕmaḥšĕbōtêkā ʾēlênû (rabbôṯ ʿāśîṯā ʾattâ YHWH ʾĕlōhay niplĕʾōṯeḵā wĕmaḥšĕḇōṯeḵā ʾēlênû): 'You have multiplied, O LORD my God, your wondrous deeds and your thoughts toward us; none can compare with you!' The declaration rabbôt ʿāśîtā ('you have done many things') introduces the theme of divine abundance: the niplĕʾôt ('wonders, marvelous deeds') and maḥšĕbôt ('thoughts, plans') toward ʾêlênû ('us') are countless. The phrase ʾên-ʿārak ʾēlêkā ('none can compare to you') is the incomparability formula — YHWH's acts are without analogue. The attempt to declare them: ʾaggîdâ wădabbĕrâ ʿāṣĕmû missappēr ('if I would declare and speak of them, they are too many to count') — the exhaustion of language before divine abundance.
6: zebaḥ ûminḥâ lōʾ-ḥāpaṣtā ʾoznayim kārîtā lî ʿōlâ waḥăṭāʾâ lōʾ šāʾāltā (zeḇaḥ ûminḥâ lōʾ-ḥāp̄aṣtā ʾoznayim kārîtā lî ʿōlâ waḥăṭāʾâ lōʾ šāʾāltā): 'In sacrifice and offering you have not delighted; ears you have dug for me; burnt offering and sin offering you have not required.' The declaration that YHWH does not ḥāpaṣ ('delight in') the four sacrifice types — zebaḥ (peace offering), minḥâ (grain offering), ʿōlâ (burnt offering), ḥaṭāʾâ (sin offering) — is prophetically radical, echoing Isaiah 1:11; Amos 5:21-22; Micah 6:6-8. The remarkable alternative: ʾoznayim kārîtā lî, 'ears you have dug for me' (MT) — possibly meaning 'you have created me as one who hears/obeys.' The LXX reads instead 'a body you have prepared for me' (sōma katērtisō moi), and Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes the LXX version, applying it to the incarnation: Christ's body is the true offering that replaces animal sacrifice.
7: ʾāz ʾāmartî hinnēh bāʾtî bimĕgillat-sēper kātûb ʿālay (ʾāz ʾāmartî hinnēh ḇāʾṯî ḇimĕgillat-sēper kāṯûḇ ʿālay): 'Then I said, 'Behold, I have come; in the scroll of the book it is written of me.'' The phrase hinnēh bāʾtî ('behold, I have come') is the response to the inadequacy of sacrifices: the psalmist comes in person, in obedience. The scroll (mĕgillat-sēper) contains what is kātûb ʿālay ('written about me') — a mysterious scroll that prescribes the speaker's role. Hebrews 10:7 applies this to Christ: 'Then I said, 'Behold, I have come to do your will, O God, as it is written of me in the scroll of the book.'' The scroll that prescribes Christ's obedience encompasses the entire OT witness to the Messiah — the Psalter's voice becomes the Messiah's self-declaration at the incarnation.
8: laʿăśôt rĕṣônĕkā ʾĕlōhay ḥāpaṣtî wĕtôrātĕkā bĕtôk mēʿāy (laʿăśôṯ rĕṣôneḵā ʾĕlōhay ḥāp̄aṣtî wĕṯôrāṯeḵā bĕṯôḵ mēʿāy): 'I delight to do your will, O my God; your law is within my heart.' The psalmist's (and in Heb 10, Christ's) self-description: laʿăśôt rĕṣônĕkā ḥāpaṣtî, 'to do your will I delight' — the ḥāpaṣ ('delight') that YHWH does not find in animal sacrifice (Psalms 40:6) is found here, in the person who delights in obedience. The parallel: wĕtôrātĕkā bĕtôk mēʿāy, 'and your Torah is in my inner parts/bowels' — Torah internalized in the very viscera (mēʿayim), not merely studied. This is the Jeremianic new covenant (Jeremiah 31:33) realized in the individual who offers obedience rather than animal blood. In Christ, this becomes the perfect incarnate obedience that accomplishes what no sacrifice could.
9: biśśartî ṣedeq bĕqāhāl rāb hinnēh śĕpātay lōʾ ʾeklaʾ YHWH ʾattâ yādāʿtā (biśśarṯî ṣeḏeq bĕqāhāl rāḇ hinnēh śĕp̄āṯay lōʾ ʾeḵlāʾ YHWH ʾattâ yāḏāʿtā): 'I have told the glad news of righteousness in the great congregation; behold, I have not restrained my lips, as you know, O LORD.' The verb bāśar (piel biśśartî, 'to bring good news, evangelize') — the root of bĕśôrâ ('good news, gospel') — makes the psalmist's public testimony explicitly an act of evangelism: the ṣedeq ('righteousness/vindication') is proclaimed as glad tidings to the qāhāl rāb ('the great assembly'). The witness is complete: lōʾ ʾeklaʾ śĕpātay ('I have not restrained my lips'). The LXX uses euēngelisamēn ('I evangelized') — the same word-group as NT euaggelion ('gospel').
10: lōʾ-kissîtî ṣidqātĕkā bĕtôk libbî ʾĕmûnātĕkā ûtĕšûʿātĕkā ʾāmartî lōʾ-kiḥadtî ḥasdĕkā waʾămittĕkā lĕqāhāl rāb (lōʾ-kissîṯî ṣiḏqāṯeḵā bĕṯôḵ libbî ʾĕmûnāṯeḵā ûṯĕšûʿāṯeḵā ʾāmartî lōʾ-ḵiḥaḏtî ḥasdĕḵā waʾămiṯṯeḵā lĕqāhāl rāḇ): 'I have not hidden your righteousness within my heart; I have spoken of your faithfulness and your salvation; I have not concealed your steadfast love and your faithfulness from the great assembly.' The five divine attributes publicly declared — ṣĕdāqâ ('righteousness'), ʾĕmûnâ ('faithfulness'), yĕšûʿâ ('salvation'), ḥesed ('steadfast love'), ʾĕmet ('truth/faithfulness') — are the content of the testimony. The double negation lōʾ kissîtî ... lōʾ kiḥadtî ('I have not hidden ... I have not concealed') frames the proclamation as total, leaving nothing of YHWH's character unexpressed. The lĕqāhāl rāb ('to the great assembly') makes the testimony fully public.
11: ʾattâ YHWH lōʾ-tiklaʾ raḥămêkā mimmennî ḥasdĕkā waʾămittĕkā tāmîd yiṣṣĕrûnî (ʾattâ YHWH lōʾ-ṯiḵlāʾ raḥămeḵā mimmennî ḥasdĕḵā waʾămiṯṯeḵā ṯāmîd yiṣṣĕrûnî): 'As for you, O LORD, you will not restrain your mercy from me; your steadfast love and your faithfulness will ever preserve me.' The transition: the psalmist declared God's character publicly (Psalms 40:9-10); now he prays that character toward himself. The verb kālaʾ ('restrain, hold back') — used in Psalms 40:9 for the psalmist's non-restraint of lips — is now inverted in prayer: ʾattâ YHWH lōʾ-tiklaʾ raḥămêkā ('you, O LORD, do not restrain your mercies from me'). The dual guardians ḥasdĕkā waʾămittĕkā ('your steadfast love and your faithfulness') tāmîd yiṣṣĕrûnî ('always preserve me') — these divine attributes are the relentless pursuers of Psalms 23:6's 'goodness and mercy shall follow me.'
12: kî ʾăpāpûnî rāʿôt ʿad-ʾên mispar tĕʾăzûqûnî ʿăwōnōtay wĕlōʾ-yākolltî lirʾôt ʿāṣĕmû miśśaʿărôt rōʾšî (kî ʾăp̄āp̄ûnî rāʿôṯ ʿaḏ-ʾên mispar ṯĕʾăzûqûnî ʿăwōnōṯay wĕlōʾ-yāḵoltî lirʾôṯ ʿāṣĕmû miśśaʿărôṯ rōʾšî): 'For evils have encompassed me beyond number; my iniquities have overtaken me, and I cannot see; they are more than the hairs of my head; my heart fails me.' The psalm pivots from praise (Psalms 40:1-11) to lament (Psalms 40:12-17). The evils (rāʿôt) that encompass (ʾāpap, 'surround, engulf') are ʿad-ʾên mispar ('without number'). The iniquities (ʿăwōnōt) that tĕʾăzûqûnî ('have overtaken, seized') — from ʾāzaq ('to seize') — are ʿāṣĕmû miśśaʿărôt rōʾšî, 'more than the hairs of my head.' The heart's failure — wĕlibî ʿăzābānî ('my heart has forsaken me') — returns to the language of Ps 38's bodily collapse. Vv.12-17 appear again in Psalm 70 as a standalone prayer for help, confirming its independent liturgical use.
13: rĕṣēh YHWH lĕhaṣṣîlēnî YHWH lĕʿezrātî ḥûšâ (rĕṣēh YHWH lĕhaṣṣîlēnî YHWH lĕʿezrātî ḥûšâ): 'Be pleased, O LORD, to deliver me! O LORD, make haste to help me!' The double imperative — rĕṣēh ('be pleased, be willing!') and ḥûšâ ('hurry!') — addresses both YHWH's will and his timing. The verb rāṣâ ('to be pleased with, accept favorably') asks YHWH to lĕhaṣṣîlēnî ('deliver me') — not from duty but from willing favor. The urgency ḥûšâ lĕʿezrātî, 'hurry to my help,' returns to Psalms 38:22's identical closing. The phrase recurs in Psalms 70:1 and shapes the ending of the Psalter's Daily Prayer tradition. In the liturgy it becomes the opening of the Hours of prayer (Deus in adiutorium meum intende).
14: yēbōšû wĕyaḥpĕrû yaḥdāw mĕbaqqĕšê napšî lispôtāh yissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyikkālĕmû ḥăpēṣê rāʿātî (yēḇōšû wĕyaḥp̄ĕrû yaḥḏāw mĕḇaqqĕšê napšî lisp̄ôṯāh yissōḡû ʾāḥôr wĕyikkālĕmû ḥăp̄ēṣê rāʿāṯî): 'Let them be put to shame and disappointed altogether who seek to snatch away my life; let them be turned back and brought to dishonor who delight in my hurt.' The imprecatory jussives — yēbōšû ('let them be ashamed'), yaḥpĕrû ('let them be confounded'), yissōgû ʾāḥôr ('let them be turned back'), yikkālĕmû ('let them be disgraced') — cover both the inner shame and the outward retreat of enemies. The phrase mĕbaqqĕšê napšî lispôtāh, 'those who seek to snatch away my life/throat,' uses lispôt ('to sweep away, snatch') — the violence of consumption imagery. The parallel ḥăpēṣê rāʿātî ('those who delight in my harm') echoes Psalms 35:26.
15: yāšōmmû ʿal-ʿēqeb buštatām hāʾōmĕrîm lî heʾāḥ heʾāḥ (yāšōmmû ʿal-ʿēqeḇ buštatām hāʾōmĕrîm lî heʾāḥ heʾāḥ): 'Let them be appalled because of their shame who say to me, 'Aha, Aha!'' The prayer yāšōmmû ('let them be desolate/appalled') — from šāmam ('to be astonished, desolate, horrified') — asks that the enemies' shame become the occasion for their own horror. The exclamation heʾāḥ heʾāḥ ('aha! aha!') — which the enemies cry at the psalmist's suffering (Psalms 35:21 also) — will rebound on those who uttered it: their gloating becomes their own shame.
16: yāśîśû wĕyiśmĕḥû bĕkā kol-mĕbaqqĕšêkā yōʾmĕrû tāmîd yigdal YHWH ʾōhăbê tĕšûʿātekā (yāśîśû wĕyiśmĕḥû ḇeḵā ḵol-mĕḇaqqĕšêḵā yōʾmĕrû ṯāmîd yiḡḏal YHWH ʾōhăḇê ṯĕšûʿāṯeḵā): 'May all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you; may those who love your salvation say continually, 'Great is the LORD!'' The counter-prayer for the covenant community: yāśîśû wĕyiśmĕḥû ('let them rejoice and be glad') — the same verbs used of the hoped-for individual joy in Psalms 40:9. The community of mĕbaqqĕšêkā ('those who seek you') and ʾōhăbê tĕšûʿātekā ('those who love your salvation') join in the permanent liturgy yigdal YHWH ('great is the LORD!'). The enemies say heʾāḥ (Psalms 40:15); the community says yigdal YHWH — the contrasting communal speech defines the two assemblies.
17: waʾănî ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ʾădōnāy yaḥăšōb lî ʿezrātî ûmĕpalṭî ʾattâ ʾĕlōhay ʾal-tĕʾaḥar (waʾănî ʿānî wĕʾeḇyôn ʾăḏōnāy yaḥăšōḇ lî ʿezrātî ûmĕp̄alṭî ʾattâ ʾĕlōhay ʾal-ṯĕʾaḥar): 'As for me, I am poor and needy, but the Lord takes thought for me. You are my help and my deliverer; do not delay, O my God!' The final verse holds the entire psalm in miniature: the psalmist is ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ('poor and needy') — the Psalter's most vulnerable pair — yet ʾădōnāy yaḥăšōb lî, 'the Lord thinks of me / takes me into account.' The verb ḥāšab ('to think, plan, account') suggests divine deliberation and intention directed toward the psalmist: YHWH calculates for him. The self-designations — ʿezrātî ('my help') and mĕpalṭî ('my deliverer') — are the psalmist's titles for YHWH. The closing prayer ʾal-tĕʾaḥar ('do not delay!') from ʾāḥar ('to tarry') urgently requests what was already promised. The psalm ends with the believer's most characteristic posture: needy, known, and waiting.
